question: What flow path is depicted in Figure 5.1-2, the RCS Simplified Diagram?

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: This section describes the evaluation of the NuScale Power Plant’s system performance, focusing on optimizing steady-state parameters such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressures to maximize electrical generation and maintain safety margins. Calculations using thermal hydraulic software and hand verification account for uncertainties and ensure low pressure losses, supporting the plant’s natural circulation design. Uncertainties related to steam generator tube plugging are not relevant due to the system’s configuration.

nuscale_ch05_0024.title: Pressurizer
nuscale_ch05_0024.summary: The pressurizer in the NuScale reactor is integrated into the upper region of the reactor vessel and functions to regulate reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure by maintaining a saturated steam-water interface. It uses heaters and spray flow to control pressure and is designed to handle operating transients without triggering unnecessary reactor trips or safety system actuations. Instrumentation provides real-time pressure and level data to plant control systems and operators.

nuscale_ch05_0023.title: Reactor Coolant System High-Point Vents
nuscale_ch05_0023.summary: This section outlines the testing and inspection requirements for the Reactor Coolant System’s DHRS (Decay Heat Removal System) components, ensuring compliance with ASME codes for Class 2 components. It specifies that DHRS actuation valves are exercised during cold shutdown conditions, and details the testing procedures—including position verification and in-situ functional testing—required to confirm proper operation and compliance with technical specifications.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain fuel integrity, minimize solid deposits, and ensure uniform boron concentration under all operating conditions, supporting both performance and safety requirements.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located inside the containment vessel and includes components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. It removes heat from the reactor core to the steam generators using natural circulation, and features systems for degasification and emergency core cooling to maintain safety and integrity. The RCS interfaces with several other systems, including chemical control, emergency cooling, and sampling systems.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and decay heat from the reactor core during non-LOCA events when normal cooling is unavailable, ensuring safe shutdown without external power. It maintains the reactor coolant system (RCS) temperature below 420°F within 36 hours, protecting fuel and pressure boundaries, and its reliability and risk significance are evaluated through established programs. The DHRS operates independently of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), but both can function together if needed.

nuscale_ch05_0002.title: Summary Description
nuscale_ch05_0002.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator, ensuring safe and efficient operation. The RCS design supports up to twelve independent modules, maintains proper coolant chemistry, and ensures uniform boron concentration and adequate mixing under all operating conditions to meet safety and performance requirements.

nuscale_ch05_0005.title: System Components
nuscale_ch05_0005.summary: This section describes key components of the NuScale reactor system. It details the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), which houses the reactor core and supports major systems, and outlines the main Reactor Coolant System (RCS) piping and its connections. The section also briefly introduces the pressurizer (PZR), emphasizing their roles as barriers and interfaces for system operations and safety functions.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the construction of the pressurizer and its components within the NuScale reactor coolant system. It specifies the types of steel and alloys used for various parts, such as the baffle plate, heater bundles, cover plate, and bolting, and confirms their compliance with relevant BPVC Section II material specifications for corrosion resistance and compatibility.

nuscale_ch05_0014.title: Reactor Vessel
nuscale_ch05_0014.summary: The Reactor Vessel section describes the NuScale Power Module's reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which houses the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer within a single vessel surrounded by a containment vessel. The RPV serves as a key pressure-retaining component and barrier to fission product release, supporting various reactor systems and components. Materials used for the RPV are selected according to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code standards to ensure integrity and safety throughout the plant's design lifetime.

edges.description: Section B describes the pressurizer's location and its flow path connection to the RCS, which is part of the system evaluated in Section A's discussion of the RCS and its components as depicted in Figure 5.1-2.

Section A describes the connection of the DHRS to the steam and feed piping for decay heat removal, while Section B details inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components including the steam piping, thus both sections are related through their discussion of the DHRS flow path and its components as depicted in the RCS diagram.

Section A discusses the system evaluation and operational criteria for the RCS and its connection to the steam generator (SG), while Section B provides the design basis and components of the RCS, with both sections connected by their focus on the configuration and function of the RCS as depicted in Figure 5.1-2.

Section A discusses the system evaluation and performance criteria of the RCS, while Section B describes specific flow paths and components (such as the degasification line and reactor vent valves) that are depicted in Figure 5.1-2, thus Section B provides the detailed flow path information referenced in the analysis question and complements the general system evaluation in Section A.

Section A describes the pressurizer's integration and flow path within the reactor vessel, while Section B provides the overall design basis of the RCS, explicitly noting the inclusion of the integral pressurizer as part of the system depicted in Figure 5.1-2.

Section A describes the physical connection and flow path between the pressurizer and the reactor coolant system, while Section B explains the functional use of this flow path for degasification and venting during normal and emergency operations.

Section A discusses inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, which are part of the broader RCS described in Section B, thus providing specific details for components within the general RCS system overview in Section B.

Section A discusses inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, including actuation valves, while Section B describes the RCS degasification line and reactor vent valves that remove noncondensible gases, connecting both sections through their focus on RCS venting components and their operational and regulatory context.

Section A provides a general overview of the RCS design and its reliance on natural circulation, while Section B specifies particular flow paths and components (such as the degasification line and reactor vent valves) that are part of the RCS described in Section A.

Section A describes the design and function of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS), while Section B explains how the DHRS connects to the steam generators (SGs) and uses them to remove decay heat from the primary coolant, thus providing specific flow path details referenced in Section A.

Section B describes the pressurizer's baffle plate providing a low-resistance flow path between the pressurizer and the RCS, which is relevant to Section A's discussion of the DHRS removing heat from the RCS, as both sections reference flow paths within the RCS system.
Section B specifies inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components described in Section A, directly connecting the operational design of the DHRS to its regulatory compliance.

Section A provides a general description of the RCS components and flow, while Section B discusses the evaluation of system performance parameters that depend on the RCS flow path depicted in Figure 5.1-2.

Section A provides a general overview of the RCS and identifies the pressurizer as an integral component, while Section B gives specific design details about the pressurizer's configuration and its flow path connection to the RCS, particularly via the baffle plate.

Section A describes the operational steps and component states (e.g., valve positions, water levels) that establish the flow path, while Section B evaluates system performance and references the connection of the DHRS to the steam and feed piping, thus both sections provide complementary information about the configuration and function of the flow path depicted in Figure 5.1-2.

Section A describes operational steps involving the pressurizer water level and venting, while Section B details the pressurizer's design and its low-resistance flow path to the RCS, connecting them through the functional flow path between the pressurizer and the RCS depicted in the diagram.

Section A describes operational steps involving the opening of vents and recirculation through the DHRS heat exchangers, while Section B specifies inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, thus Section B provides regulatory specifications for the components and actions described in Section A.

Section A details the components and materials of the pressurizer, which is a part of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) depicted in Figure 5.1-2, while Section B evaluates the overall RCS performance and references the steam generators and decay heat removal system, both of which are also elements shown in the RCS diagram, thus both sections provide information about subsystems and components that together define the flow path in the RCS diagram.

Section A provides detailed material specifications for pressurizer components that are generally described in Section B's overview of the pressurizer's structure and function, specifically referencing the baffle plate and its role in the flow path between the pressurizer and the RCS.

Section A describes the physical components and materials of the pressurizer, which is part of the RCS flow path depicted in Figure 5.1-2, while Section B discusses inspection requirements for DHRS components that are also part of the RCS and may be represented in the same diagram, thus both sections provide details about different subsystems/components within the overall RCS flow path.

Section A describes the Decay Heat Removal System's function to cool the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) during specific events, while Section B provides a general overview of the RCS and its components, establishing that the DHRS operates on and interfaces with the RCS as depicted in Figure 5.1-2.

Section A describes the function and design basis of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) in removing decay heat from the Reactor Coolant System (RCS), while Section B details operational steps and system components, including the use of DHRS heat exchangers and isolation of steam generators, thus Section B provides specific operational context and component actions that implement the general DHRS function described in Section A.

Section B provides cross-references to other sections that describe the materials and components of the pressurizer, which is a part of the Reactor Coolant System discussed in Section A's context of decay heat removal.

Section A provides a general overview of the RCS and its reliance on natural circulation, while Section B details specific operational steps and components (such as the DHRS heat exchangers, SGs, PZR, and degasification line) that are part of the flow path depicted in Figure 5.1-2.

Section A provides a general overview of the RCS and its components, while Section B specifies detailed information about the pressurizer materials, which is one of the components mentioned in Section A.

Section A describes operational steps involving the pressurizer and its integration with the reactor coolant system, while Section B provides detailed material specifications for the pressurizer components referenced in Section A.

Section A describes the physical components and configuration of the Reactor Vessel within the Reactor Coolant System (RCS), while Section B discusses the operational evaluation and performance criteria of the RCS, connecting them through their shared focus on the RCS's structure and function.

Section B explains that the pressurizer is an integral part of the reactor vessel described in Section A, specifically detailing how the upper region of the reactor vessel functions as the pressurizer and how a baffle plate provides a flow path between the pressurizer and the RCS.

Section A describes the overall configuration and components of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS), while Section B details inspection and classification requirements for specific RCS components such as the DHRS actuation valves, connecting the general system layout in A to the specific component requirements in B.
